prompt:
In a review of a naturalist’s journal, a critic says the writer’s style is “plain”: observations are described directly, with little ornament or dramatic emphasis.
As used in the text, what does “plain” most nearly mean?

choices.A: simple and direct
choices.B: deeply emotional
choices.C: playfully sarcastic
choices.D: unusually technical

explanation: The passage contrasts the style with ornament and drama, so “plain” means simple and direct.

prompt:
Some schools have added gravel-filled trenches beside playground pavement. During storms, the trenches collect runoff that would otherwise pool on the blacktop, and the water slowly filters into the ground below. As a result, the pavement dries faster after rain.
Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

choices.A: Gravel trenches can reduce puddling on playground pavement by collecting and absorbing runoff
choices.B: Playgrounds should be covered entirely with gravel instead of pavement
choices.C: Stormwater always damages playgrounds unless it is pumped away immediately
choices.D: Schools add trenches mainly to make playgrounds look more natural

explanation: The passage focuses on trenches collecting runoff so pavement dries faster.

prompt:
Before bakeries began using stackable delivery crates of the same size, grocery clerks often had to unload bread by hand because crates from different suppliers would not fit together securely. Once standard crate sizes became common, stores could move deliveries more efficiently.
Which choice can most reasonably be inferred from the text?

choices.A: Standard crate sizes made bakery deliveries easier for stores to handle
choices.B: Grocery clerks preferred unloading bread by hand even after crates were standardized
choices.C: All bakeries adopted identical crate sizes at the same time
choices.D: Standardized crates eliminated the need to check deliveries for accuracy

explanation: The text says standard sizes let stores move deliveries more efficiently.

prompt:
An archaeologist notices that the stones in the center of a ruin’s courtyard are more deeply worn than those along the edges. She points to that pattern as evidence that people once crossed the middle of the courtyard frequently.
Which choice best describes the function of the first sentence in the text?

choices.A: It presents physical evidence that the second sentence interprets
choices.B: It introduces a theory that the second sentence rejects
choices.C: It explains why the courtyard is difficult to excavate
choices.D: It compares two ruins that the second sentence later contrasts

explanation: The worn stones are the evidence, and the second sentence explains what that evidence suggests.

prompt:
A student is writing about free sewing kits at neighborhood repair cafés and has taken these notes:
• Some repair cafés give visitors small sewing kits with needles, thread, and buttons
• People can use the kits to mend loose hems or missing buttons at home
• First-time menders may not already own sewing supplies
• The kits can make simple clothing repairs easier to try
The student wants to emphasize how the kits help beginners attempt small repairs. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.A: Some repair cafés give visitors small sewing kits with needles, thread, and buttons
choices.B: First-time menders may not already own sewing supplies
choices.C: Free sewing kits can make simple clothing repairs easier for beginners by providing supplies they may not already have
choices.D: People can use the kits to mend loose hems or missing buttons at home

explanation: This choice directly combines the ideas of beginner access and missing supplies.

prompt:
The town added a second scanner at the entrance to its recreation center. ______, people can check in more quickly before evening classes.
Which choice completes the text with the most logical transition?

choices.A: For example,
choices.B: As a result,
choices.C: Meanwhile,
choices.D: Otherwise,

explanation: The quicker check-in is the result of adding a second scanner.

prompt:
The archive’s smallest globe, <u>mounted on an oak stand in 1791,</u> sits in a climate-controlled display case.
Which choice best punctuates the underlined portion of the text?

choices.A: NO CHANGE
choices.B: ; mounted on an oak stand in 1791;
choices.C: : mounted on an oak stand in 1791:
choices.D: —mounted on an oak stand in 1791—

explanation: The phrase is nonessential information and is correctly set off with commas.

prompt:
The feature article praises the chef’s inventive menus, <u>it says little about the assistants who prepared ingredients before service began.</u>
Which choice most effectively revises the underlined portion of the text to maintain the grammatical correctness of the sentence?

choices.A: it says little about the assistants who prepared ingredients before service began
choices.B: and it says little about the assistants who prepared ingredients before service began
choices.C: but it says little about the assistants who prepared ingredients before service began
choices.D: saying little about the assistants who prepared ingredients before service began

explanation: The sentence contains two independent clauses and needs a conjunction; “but” correctly signals contrast.

prompt:
The revised schedule was sent only to Lucia and <u>I</u> before the rehearsal began.
Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.A: I
choices.B: me
choices.C: my
choices.D: myself

explanation: The pronoun is part of the compound object of the preposition “to.”

prompt:
Since 2018, the community arts center <u>offers</u> free drawing workshops every spring.
Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.A: offers
choices.B: offered
choices.C: has offered
choices.D: will offer

explanation: Since 2018 calls for the present perfect tense.

prompt:
After reviewing the flyer one last time, <u>the missing date was obvious to Amir.</u>
Which choice best revises the text to ensure that the modifying phrase is correctly placed?

choices.A: the missing date was obvious to Amir
choices.B: Amir noticed that the date was missing
choices.C: the flyer made the missing date obvious to Amir
choices.D: obvious to Amir, the missing date appeared after the review

explanation: The opening phrase describes Amir’s action, so Amir should be the subject of the main clause.

prompt:
[1] Some rail stations now keep shelves of free city maps near their exits. [2] Travelers can take a map while deciding how to reach a hotel or museum. [3] Because the shelves are placed where people already pause, picking up a map requires little extra effort. [4] In some stations, transit staff update the maps monthly to reflect detours.
Which choice best describes the most logical placement for the following sentence?
“The shelves can turn a quick stop at the station into a brief moment of trip planning.”

choices.A: Before sentence 1
choices.B: After sentence 2
choices.C: After sentence 3
choices.D: After sentence 4

explanation: The sentence best follows the explanation that taking a map requires little effort and summarizes the shelves’ benefit.

prompt:
A teacher recorded the average number of sample cards four student groups sorted in ten minutes.
Group A: 16
Group B: 21
Group C: 18
Group D: 17
Which choice is best supported by the data?

choices.A: Group A sorted more cards, on average, than Group C
choices.B: Group B sorted the most cards, on average
choices.C: Group C and Group D sorted the same average number of cards
choices.D: The average number of cards sorted increased steadily from Group A through Group D

explanation: Group B’s 21 cards is the highest value in the data.

prompt:
The reviewer calls the guide “lucid,” noting that it makes a complicated process easy to follow.
As used in the text, what does “lucid” most nearly mean?

choices.A: clear
choices.B: skeptical
choices.C: ornamental
choices.D: fragmentary

explanation: The passage says the guide is easy to follow, so “lucid” means clear.

prompt:
In a short story, the narrator says, “My grandmother kept the brass bakery stamp in a drawer beside the stove long after the shop closed. It marked no loaves anymore, but when she lifted it, the kitchen seemed to wake before dawn again.”
Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

choices.A: The stamp preserves a connection to the grandmother’s former work and routine
choices.B: The narrator plans to reopen the bakery with the old stamp
choices.C: The drawer matters only because the stamp is stored there
choices.D: The grandmother keeps the stamp because it is valuable metal

explanation: The object no longer functions, but it still symbolizes the grandmother’s earlier work.

prompt:
In an experiment, young tomato plants grown beside reflective white mulch developed straighter stems than plants grown beside dark bark mulch. The researchers suggest that the lighter mulch reflected additional light onto the shaded sides of the plants.
Which choice is best supported by the text?

choices.A: Reflective white mulch may help tomato plants grow straighter by increasing side light
choices.B: Tomato plants can grow properly only with reflective mulch
choices.C: Dark bark mulch always prevents tomatoes from receiving enough light
choices.D: The experiment proved that mulch matters more than water in every situation

explanation: The passage directly links straighter growth to extra reflected light from the white mulch.

prompt:
A local reporter writes, “The block’s barbershop was also its unofficial opinion column.”
Why does the author most likely include the quotation in the text?

choices.A: To present one reporter’s view of the barbershop as a place where people exchanged commentary and opinions
choices.B: To prove that newspapers were sold only in barbershops
choices.C: To explain how barbershops were designed to reduce noise
choices.D: To describe the writing style of a particular column

explanation: The quotation presents the barbershop as a site of conversation and opinion-sharing.

prompt:
A student is writing about free field notebooks at nature centers and has taken these notes:
• Some nature centers lend small notebooks and pencils to visitors
• Visitors can use them to record bird sightings or sketch leaves
• Prompt cards suggest details to notice, such as color, shape, and habitat
• The materials can make close observation easier for beginners
The student wants to make a general claim about how the notebooks help new visitors. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes?

choices.A: Field notebooks can help beginners observe nature more closely by providing materials and simple prompts
choices.B: Some nature centers lend small notebooks and pencils to visitors
choices.C: Visitors can use the notebooks to record bird sightings or sketch leaves
choices.D: Prompt cards are useful only for visitors who already know plant and bird names

explanation: This choice combines the notes about supplied materials, prompts, and beginner support.

prompt:
The article begins with handwritten claim tickets once used by cloakrooms in train stations. ______, it turns to the numbered metal tags that later replaced them.
Which choice completes the text with the most logical transition?

choices.A: Similarly,
choices.B: Later,
choices.C: Otherwise,
choices.D: For example,

explanation: The passage moves from an earlier practice to what replaced it later.

prompt:
The internship asked Mei to sort invoices, label boxes, and <u>record shipping dates.</u>
Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.A: recording shipping dates
choices.B: record shipping dates
choices.C: recorded shipping dates
choices.D: to record shipping dates

explanation: The sentence requires parallel base-form verbs: sort, label, and record.

prompt:
The editors’ meeting room is beside the archive on the second floor.
Which choice best explains the use of the apostrophe in the underlined word?

choices.A: It shows that the room belongs to or is associated with more than one editor
choices.B: It marks the plural form of a noun that is not possessive
choices.C: It shows that the second floor belongs to the editors
choices.D: It is used incorrectly because the noun should be singular

explanation: The apostrophe after the plural noun marks plural possession or association.

prompt:
A basket of apricots and plums <u>sits</u> on the table near the window.
Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.A: sit
choices.B: sits
choices.C: have sat
choices.D: sitting

explanation: The singular subject “basket” takes the singular verb “sits.”

prompt:
A student wrote the following sentence in a draft:
“When the council postponed the vote, this upset several residents.”
Which choice best explains why the sentence should be revised?

choices.A: The sentence should be revised because the pronoun “this” has an unclear antecedent
choices.B: The sentence should be revised because councils should not postpone votes
choices.C: The sentence should be revised because the verb should be in the future tense
choices.D: The sentence should be revised because residents should not be mentioned in formal writing

explanation: It is unclear what “this” refers to in the sentence.

prompt:
[1] Some apartment buildings now keep small shelves for cookbook swaps in the lobby. [2] Residents can leave a cookbook they no longer use and take another one home. [3] Because the shelves are near the mailboxes, people often browse them during routines they already follow. [4] In some buildings, residents add sticky notes recommending favorite recipes.
Which choice best describes the most logical placement for the following sentence?
“The shelves can turn a quick stop in the lobby into a small exchange of reading and cooking ideas.”

choices.A: Before sentence 1
choices.B: After sentence 2
choices.C: After sentence 3
choices.D: After sentence 4

explanation: The added sentence best follows the description of browsing during routine stops and summarizes the shelves’ purpose.

prompt:
To make the sentence more concise, the editor replaced “in the event that” with <u>if</u>.
Which choice best describes the effect of the revision?

choices.A: It changes the meaning by making the condition less clear
choices.B: It shortens the sentence without changing its meaning
choices.C: It makes the sentence less grammatical
choices.D: It introduces a more formal tone

explanation: “If” is a shorter equivalent of “in the event that.”

prompt:
In an experiment, museum visitors identifying carved stone tools made fewer mistakes when display cards included profile silhouettes in addition to front-view photographs than when the cards included front views alone.
Which choice is best supported by the text?

choices.A: Additional profile silhouettes may help visitors identify carved stone tools more accurately
choices.B: Front-view photographs are unnecessary in all stone-tool displays
choices.C: The visitors had memorized all the tools before the experiment
choices.D: Profile silhouettes are more important than every other visual feature in all cases

explanation: The passage directly reports fewer errors when profile silhouettes were included.

prompt:
An article about restored tollhouses explains that buildings once used to collect fees from travelers are now often repaired and opened to visitors. The article ends by noting that some schools use the buildings during field trips on roads, trade, and local government.
Which choice best describes the function of the final sentence in the text?

choices.A: It provides an example of a present-day educational use connected to the restored buildings
choices.B: It argues that all schools should study roads through field trips
choices.C: It shifts the discussion away from restoration to unrelated tourism data
choices.D: It denies that restored tollhouses have any public value

explanation:
The final sentence gives a current school use for the restored tollhouses.
PART D — MODULE 2 ANSWER KEY + EXPLANATIONS
